exemplar.title: To what extent does director Bong Joon-Ho represent family and class in âThe Hostâ and âSnowpiercerâ using visual symbolism and settings? â

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does director Bong Joon-Ho represent family and class in âThe Hostâ and âSnowpiercerâ using visual symbolism and settings? â

strengths: Topic and purpose are clearly communicated in the title and introduction
Research question is explicitly stated and linked to film form and themes
Methodology employs appropriate textual analysis and secondary criticism sources
weaknesses: Scope is overly broad, with digressions to films outside the research question
Methodology lacks justification of source selection and systematic analytical procedure
Citation style is uneven and in-text integration of sources is weak
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work consistently fits the 3â4 band: the topic and research question are communicated adequately, and methods are appropriate, but focus drifts to off-scope films and methodological justification is incomplete. These mid-band characteristics warrant 3 marks out of 6.

strengths: Generally accurate use of film terminology (e.g., low-angle, birdâs-eye view)
Sound awareness of mise-en-scÃ¨ne, camera angles, lighting, and symbolic spaces
Effective application of technical analysis in key scenes (lighting contrasts, train hierarchy)
weaknesses: Analysis is often descriptive rather than critical, with limited interrogation of sources
Content occasionally drifts to unrelated films, undermining subject coherence
Precision of terminology falters at times and definitions lack scholarly grounding
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Performance sits solidly in the 3â4 band: clear knowledge of film concepts and mostly correct terminology, but understanding is partial, source application is rarely interrogated, and analysis sometimes drifts off-topic. A score of 3 out of 6 reflects these strengths and limitations.

strengths: Some analytical passages convincingly link camera angles and lighting to themes of power and class
Comparative close-up analysis of Curtis and Wilford supports the thematic argument
Nuanced reading of the monster metaphor deepens critical evaluation
weaknesses: Evaluation is surface-level and repetitive with few counter-arguments or alternative readings
Arguments are occasionally unsupported and digress into other films, weakening logical progression
Conclusion omits discussion of limitations or wider implications, reducing critical depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay meets the lower end of the 4â6 band: research goes beyond minimal, and there is evidence of interpretation, but analysis is inconsistent, evaluation is modest, and alternative perspectives are underdeveloped. Awarding 4 out of 12 reflects this partial critical thinking.

strengths: Basic extended essay structure is present with title page, contents, introduction, body, conclusion, and works cited
Clear section headings (e.g., Cinematic Techniques, Character Development) aid orientation
Figures are captioned and sometimes well integrated with the text
weaknesses: Formatting is inconsistent: font sizes, spacing, and heading styles vary
Pagination is unclear and citation style in the works cited is uneven
Low-resolution and duplicated images disrupt readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation clearly falls in the 1â2 band: all required elements are present, but structural and formatting inconsistencies and poor visual quality limit effectiveness. A score of 2 out of 4 accurately reflects this level.

strandSelections.reasoning:
â¢ The essay includes a title, word count and a contents page, and images are captioned, indicating some attention to presentation.  
â¢ However, **MLA/APA conventions are inconsistently applied**, font sizes and spacing fluctuate, and several images are low resolution or duplicated, detracting from readability. Page numbering is not evident in the extract.  
â¢ Given these shortcomings, layout/formatting meets requirements only partially, placing the work in the 1-2 band (score 2).

â¢ **Topic communication** â The essay identifies a legitimate film topic â Bong Joon-hoâs representation of family and class in The Host and Snowpiercer â and this is signalled in the title, the opening quotation and the introduction.  
â¢ However, the focus is **only partially sustained**: frequent digressions to Parasite and Mother blur the central line of inquiry and indicate that the scope is broader than the essay can manage within 4 000 words.  
â¢ Overall, the topic and purpose are communicated adequately but with periodic loss of precision; this aligns with the 3-4 band.

â¢ The bibliography lists a **moderate range of sources** (critical essays, interviews, reviews, blogs) and the body cites several of them.  
â¢ Relevance to the question is evident but **uneven**; some citations pertain to Parasite or to general colour theory rather than the set films, and key academic film scholarship is sparse.  
â¢ Overall, research goes beyond minimal but lacks the breadth, depth and discrimination required for higher bands, fitting the lower end of 4-6 (score 4).

â¢ The student outlines a basic methodology (consulting reviews, interviews, and publications) and cites a range of secondary sources plus frame stills from the films.  
â¢ Evidence of **informed selection is inconsistent**: some sources are reputable (Kermode, Ebert), but others are general websites or blogs with limited academic weight; citation style is uneven and in-text integration is weak.  
â¢ The chosen methods (textual analysis combined with secondary criticism) are appropriate but not thoroughly justified or applied systematically, matching the mid-band (3-4).

â¢ The discussion shows an **attempt to evaluate** Bong Joon-hoâs effectiveness and to compare the two films, and a brief conclusion draws findings back to the research question.  
â¢ However, evaluation is **surface-level and repetitive**, with limited consideration of counter-arguments or alternative readings; synthesis between sources and film evidence is modest.  
â¢ The argument is generally discernible but lacks depth and critical edge, meriting a score at the bottom of the 4-6 band.

â¢ A basic EE structure (title page, contents, introduction, body, conclusion, works cited) is present, satisfying minimum requirements.  
â¢ Nevertheless, internal organisation is **uneven**: headings are inconsistently formatted, some sections (e.g., âBodyâ) are overly broad, and visual material is inserted with captions that disrupt flow.  
â¢ Because the structure is only partially effective and some required elements (consistent pagination, clear sub-sectioning) are weak, the work sits in the 1-2 band (score 2).

â¢ The essay shows **sound awareness of key film concepts** such as mise-en-scÃ¨ne, camera angles and lighting; numerous examples from both films are mentioned.  
â¢ Nevertheless, much of the writing is descriptive (âBong uses low angles to show powerâ) and occasionally drifts into unrelated films (Parasite, Mother), suggesting **partial rather than thorough** subject mastery.  
â¢ Source material is quoted but seldom critically interrogated; links to the research question are intermittent, so performance aligns with the 3-4 band (score 3).

â¢ The essay offers **some analytical passages**, e.g., linking camera angles to power dynamics and lighting to social hierarchy, indicating an attempt at interpretive reasoning.  
â¢ Yet much content remains descriptive (plot retelling, catalogue of techniques), and arguments are sometimes asserted without adequate evidence or comparison across the two films. Logical progression suffers from digressions to other works.  
â¢ Thus analysis is present but only partially developed and inconsistently supported, corresponding to the 4-6 band at its lower end (score 4).

â¢ The research question is **explicitly stated** and clearly linked to film form (visual symbolism and setting) and thematic concerns (family and class).  
â¢ Yet the phrasing âto what extentâ across two feature films invites an **overly broad comparison**, and the ensuing discussion does not maintain a tight analytical line; sections drift into general description of cinematography or summaries of plot.  
â¢ Because the question is clear but only partially focused and not always carried through the analysis, performance best fits the 3-4 band.

â¢ Film terminology (e.g., âlow-angle,â âbirdâs-eye view,â ânatural vs. artificial lightingâ) is used regularly and usually correctly, demonstrating **generally accurate command** of key concepts.  
â¢ However, precision falters at points (e.g., confusing shot/reverse-shot with over-the-shoulder, mis-labelling actors, uneven discussion of symbolism), and definitions are rarely substantiated with scholarly references.  
â¢ Consistency and depth are therefore mixed; the work meets but does not surpass the expectations of the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The investigation title is presented clearly and concisely at the start, guiding the reader into the topic and meeting formal report requirements.
The research question uses âto what extentâ across two films, creating an overly broad scope that may dilute the analysis; consider narrowing the focus or splitting into sub-questions.
The introduction restates the research question but lacks an outline of the essayâs structure or analytical roadmap; add a brief overview of key sections and methods.
The methodology describes general source types but does not justify selection criteria or explain a systematic approach to film analysis; specify how data will be collected and analyzed.
The clear âCinematic Techniquesâ heading effectively signals a shift into the analytical body of the essay, aiding reader orientation.
Figure 1 is well integrated with a descriptive caption, enhancing the readerâs understanding of the camera angle contrast.
The word count is clearly stated and adheres to the 4000-word limit requirement.
The table of contents is present but section indentation and formatting are inconsistent; adopt uniform styling to improve navigability.
The introduction includes summaries of Parasite and Mother, which fall outside the defined films; maintain focus on The Host and Snowpiercer to strengthen coherence.
A quotation from Megan Todd is included without critical interrogation or assessment of relevance; integrate and evaluate sources to demonstrate deeper subject understanding.
Claims about low- and high-angle effects are asserted without precise scene references or timestamps; include exact examples to support analytical points.
A quote about âbroken codesâ is introduced without follow-up analysis; explain how this concept relates to your close-up discussion to deepen evaluation.
The close-up analysis largely describes what is visible; strengthen it by linking emotional impact directly to themes of family and class.
The analysis of Snowpiercerâs angles repeats earlier observations without advancing the argument; consolidate redundant points and focus on new insights.
Terminology such as âbirdâs eye viewâ and âover-the-shoulderâ is used without theoretical grounding; cite a film studies source to define these shot types.
The comparative close-up analysis of Curtis and Wilford cogently contrasts emotional states, effectively supporting your thematic argument.
The discussion shifts to Parasite, which is outside the scope of your research question; focus analysis exclusively on The Host and Snowpiercer.
Including Figure 6 from Parasite introduces scope creep; remove or replace with examples from the two target films.
The discussion of lighting contrasts and costume in the hospital scene insightfully links technical choices to class symbolism.
The âCharacter development and Relationshipsâ heading clearly signposts the thematic shift and aids structural coherence.
The family dynamics analysis is descriptive; reinforce it by explicitly connecting developments in relationships to your research question on class representation.
The later character development discussion revisits earlier points without adding depth; consider consolidating to streamline your argument.
Figure 10 effectively visualizes Curtisâs emergence as a leader, reinforcing your discussion of character transformation.
Figure 11 from Mother falls outside your defined film pair; focus only on The Host and Snowpiercer to maintain research precision.
The opening of the settings section offers a coherent thematic introduction linking visual symbolism to social class and family dynamics.
The analysis of the lower frames in Figure 12 is implied but not articulated; explicitly discuss how the hospital corridor and sewer images function as symbolic spaces.
The discussion of marginalization through the familyâs trailer, hospital, and sewer settings persuasively links physical spaces to socio-economic status.
Analysis of the monster as a metaphor for environmental and social anxieties offers a nuanced reading that deepens critical evaluation.
The vivid portrayal of train hierarchy symbolism links the physical layout of Snowpiercer to themes of class division and power dynamics.
Figure 14 from Parasite again extends beyond your chosen films; remove this off-scope example to sharpen focus.
The conclusion summarizes observations but omits discussion of study limitations or implications; include reflective evaluation to reinforce critical thinking.
The Works Cited list shows inconsistent formatting and ordering; apply a single citation style and alphabetize entries for clarity.
